_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve maps Treasury yields across maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. Its slope indicates market expectations: a normal upward slope suggests higher yields for longer maturities due to risks like inflation and time, while an inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded recessions. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.38% with a 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread positive at 0.3 points, reflecting an upward slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the 2s10s spread was inverted, reaching a series low of -0.2 points, indicating a downward-sloping curve then.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a widely followed recession indicator, with negative values historically preceding recessions by 6 to 18 months. The spread was inverted from 2022 through 2024 but has since un-inverted, rising to +0.3 points, signaling some steepening of the curve. However, the New York Fed's recession probability model assigns a 31% chance of recession, reflecting lingering economic uncertainty. It's important to note that the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before an actual recession arrives, so a positive 2s10s spread does not eliminate recession risks.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve’s positive slope and un-inverted 2s10s spread suggest the immediate recession signal from the curve has attenuated compared to the recent inversion period. Combined with a moderate 31% recession probability from the NY Fed, the curve signals caution but not imminent contraction. Investors should consider this in a broader context beyond slope alone.
